SUMMARY INFORMATION.Application Title: The GuideALIGN Study: A data-driven approach to monitoring the implementation of NICE guidance

APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary of Research:
Why is this research needed?
Clinical guidelines are recommendations on how to diagnose and treat medical conditions, based upon the best
available evidence. However, we cannot easily check whether these recommendations are being followed in real-
world care. Current methods (e.g. collecting data manually using spreadsheets) take too long and make it hard to
build a national picture of care quality.
Importantly, we can now bring together electronic health records for the whole population of England using a
system called OpenSAFELY. This does not need manual data collection, and patients cannot be identified. It gives
us a unique opportunity to change how we monitor the quality of care for patients.
What are the aims of my research?
My goal is to improve patient care by increasing the use of guideline recommendations. To achieve this, I will do
the following:
1. Create a data-driven way of monitoring guideline use in real-world care;
2. Use this information to make a plan that gives guideline-recommended care to patients;
3. Test this plan using real-world data in OpenSAFELY.
To show this, I have chosen a condition with very effective treatments, where guidelines are rarely followed. Gout
is a common type of arthritis, affecting 1 in 40 adults. It causes severe pain, permanent joint damage and health
problems (e.g. kidney disease) if not treated well enough. Very good medications (e.g. allopurinol) can prevent
these complications, but they are not used often enough or in the right amounts.
What will my project do?
1. Check guideline use: I will use anonymous GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY to
see whether national treatment recommendations are being followed for people with gout. I will show how
care varies throughout England; find out what determines whether patients receive good quality care; and
how this affects health outcomes.
2. Make a plan to improve care: I will work with patients, healthcare professionals, charities and policy
makers, to find areas of good care and areas for improvement. Together, we will make a plan that gives
guideline-recommended care to people with gout, using digital solutions and education.
3. Real-world testing: I will test whether this plan improves patient care using an efficient, real-world study in
OpenSAFELY. General practices (38 in total) will provide care to people with gout using either the new
plan or the treatment that would usually be given. All study data will be collected automatically in
OpenSAFELY, with no extra data needed from patients or doctors. The following results will be compared
for patients treated for gout over a 12-month period:
Whether patients received guideline-recommended treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
o
If they developed complications of gout (e.g. kidney problems);
o
o How often they visited their GP or hospital.
How will the results be used?
My research will directly benefit people with gout, by reducing pain and disability. It will help doctors and nurses
give better care to their patients. It will reduce costs for the NHS and help focus resources on areas where
improvement is needed most. I will be working closely with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
(NICE) to make sure my research benefits people with other long-term conditions. I will share all of my findings
openly through conferences, research papers, and by working with our professional and patient communities.
Patient and public involvement (PPI)
My research plan has been shaped by the experiences of patients, gathered through interviews, patient and carer
events, and discussions with patient organisations. Throughout the project, I will be working together with patient
partners, a patient and lay members group and the UK Gout Society, to create participant resources, review my
findings, and support PPI strategy.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
Background
The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) has published over 300 clinical guidelines, containing
evidence-based recommendations for best practice care. However, the ability to monitor real-world uptake of these
recommendations and demonstrate impact for patients is limited. Existing approaches rely upon manual data
collection, which is time-consuming and provides only a snapshot of performance. This makes it challenging to
detect variation in care and target interventions towards reducing healthcare inequality.
Many of these limitations could be overcome by using electronic health data that are captured routinely in clinical
practice. Through the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment, we now have the ability to analyse health
data in near real-time for 58 million people, covering more than 99% of England’s population. This creates the
potential for continuous evaluation of care quality across multiple disease areas.
Aims and objectives
I will develop a data-driven approach to monitoring the uptake of NICE guidance, demonstrating real-world impact
for patients, and facilitating efficient evaluation of quality improvement interventions. To develop this methodology,
I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-practice gap.
I will complete three workstreams:
1. Develop a reproducible analytic framework to monitor the implementation of NICE guidance;
2. Design a multi-faceted intervention to maximise uptake of guideline-recommended care;
3. Evaluate this intervention using a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
Methods (timelines for delivery)
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Using anonymised GP and hospital data for 58 million people in OpenSAFELY, I will
systematically map recommendations from the NICE Gout Guideline and analyse temporal trends in their
implementation. I will use regression-modelling approaches to identify predictors of sub-optimal care, and evaluate
the impact on outcomes including comorbidities and hospitalisations. Data-driven dashboards will feedback
performance against NICE standards at national, regional and local levels.
Workstream 2 (months 12-24): I will convene focus groups with broad stakeholder representation to identify
barriers and facilitators of optimal gout care. I will collaborate with implementation science experts at King's
College London to develop an evidence-based intervention package that provides NICE-recommended care for
people with gout, incorporating digital solutions within electronic health record software and educational content.
Workstream 3 (months 24-60): I will conduct a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY to evaluate
the intervention from Workstream 2. General practices (n=38) will be randomised 1:1 to receive this intervention or
usual care. All study data will be automatically captured in OpenSAFELY and analysed using the framework
developed in Workstream 1. Outcomes will include:
 The uptake of NICE-recommended gout treatments (e.g. allopurinol);
 The incidence of gout-associated comorbidities (e.g. kidney disease);
 Consultation/hospitalisation rates.
Anticipated impact
My research will directly benefit patients with gout, by reducing pain and disability. Better disease control will
prevent avoidable hospitalisations and complications. For policy makers, it will help target strategies towards
reducing healthcare inequality. I will be collaborating with NICE throughout my Fellowship to ensure this
methodology is readily adaptable for other guidelines and diseases. I will share all of my analysis code openly, and
communicate my findings through conferences, research papers, social media, and PPIE events.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Detailed Research Plan:
1. Problem
Clinical guidelines summarise evidence-based recommendations on best practice care. However, these
recommendations are often slow to be adopted in practice,1,2,26 and we are limited in our ability to monitor the
quality of care provided to patients. Existing mechanisms rely upon manual clinical audit,3 which is time-
consuming and biased towards departments with greater resources.4 Local audits are sporadically performed and
rarely published, while national audit programmes are lacking for many conditions.
We face similar challenges when evaluating strategies designed to improve the quality of care. Trials are
costly and take years to produce results. Outcome data are usually manually collected, which is burdensome for
participants and researchers. Moreover, trial settings may not adequately reflect real-world practice.
My Fellowship seeks to address these limitations by harnessing electronic health record data that are captured
routinely during clinical care. I will generate the methodology to evaluate care quality in near real-time
without any need for manual data collection. This will enable us to quantify the impact of guideline-
recommended care on patient outcomes, and analyse factors associated with sub-optimal care. I will demonstrate
how a data-enabled trial can evaluate quality improvement interventions efficiently using entirely routinely-
collected data. My proposal aligns closely with NIHR’s vision to harness data-enabled trials,5 and NICE’s strategy
for data-driven guideline evaluation.6
To achieve these aims, I will use the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment. Through OpenSAFELY,
we can now analyse electronic health data in near real-time for 58 million people, covering >99% of England’s
population. Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other data platform: no individual patient data are visible
to researchers, and all analysis code and platform activity are publicly logged.
To develop this methodology, I have chosen gout as an exemplar disease with a persistent evidence-
practice gap. Gout is a common inflammatory disease affecting 1 in 40 adults, characterised by severe joint pain,
disability and early mortality.7 It is a hallmark of the metabolic syndrome, with patients often experiencing multiple
long-term conditions, including chronic kidney and cardiovascular disease.8,9 There are highly-effective, urate-
lowering therapies (ULT, e.g. allopurinol) that can cure symptoms and prevent complications. However, in my
NIHR Doctoral research, I showed that only one in three people with gout in the UK are prescribed these
treatments.10 The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recently published its first guideline for
the management of gout, with the aim of improving care.11 Current limitations mean that we do not know whether
these recommendations are being followed or if they have improved patient outcomes.
Using this guideline as proof-of-principle, I will develop a data-driven framework that enables us to:
i. Monitor real-world uptake of guideline recommendations;
ii. Quantify the impact for patients;
iii. Evaluate interventions designed to deliver best practice care.
2. Importance
I am collaborating with NICE to ensure the methodology I develop is adaptable across multiple guidelines and
diseases. My proposal will enable continuous evaluation of care quality, serving as an exemplar for other
healthcare systems worldwide. This capability is essential, not only to NICE and policy makers, but to the 28
National Clinical Audit programmes in England and Wales that rely heavily upon manual data. We know that data
gaps exist for under-served groups and under-resourced departments.4 By utilising routinely-captured data, we
can quality assure care more comprehensively and target interventions towards reducing healthcare inequality.
By developing this methodology for a disease with persistently sub-standard care, patients will directly benefit
from my research. Of 1.7 million people with gout in the UK, only a third are prescribed guideline-recommended
treatments.10,12 I will develop an intervention that delivers best practice care to patients, resulting in less pain,
fewer complications, and improved quality-of-life.
For healthcare professionals, the intervention will reduce unnecessary workload and enable them to achieve
better outcomes for their patients. Electronic dashboards will provide feedback on performance against NICE
standards – a powerful motivator for change.13 Better disease control will prevent avoidable hospitalisations and
complications, producing cost-savings for the NHS.14
The data-enabled trial design I am utilising will provide researchers with an efficient mechanism through which
complex interventions can be evaluated in real-world care. I will share my analysis code and methodology
openly to support efficient re-use.
3. Existing evidence
Existing approaches for monitoring the real-world uptake of guideline recommendations rely heavily upon manual
audit,3 performance.15,16
clinical which is labour-intensive and provides only a snapshot of National Audit
programmes can help to build a national picture of care quality, but with several limitations:
 No data are captured on patients not enrolled in these audits;1

 Missing data varies markedly by healthcare provider, and associates with poor care
 National Audit programmes are absent for many conditions (e.g. gout).
Electronic health record data can overcome many of these limitations. Clinically-coded information on
diagnoses, investigations and prescriptions can be linked across multiple data sources and analysed to evaluate
care quality. Existing data platforms (e.g. CPRD) have been available for over 20 years; however, their suitability
for evaluating guideline implementation is limited: data are often outdated (e.g. >2 years for hospital data) and are
present for only a minority of the population.18,19
The introduction of the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment during the COVID-19 pandemic has
transformed our ability to deliver health data analyses for the whole of England:
 There is data coverage for >99% of England’s population;20
 Primary care data are updated weekly, and secondary care data monthly;20
 There is linkage to numerous additional datasets, including high-cost drugs and referral-to-treatment
times;20
 Privacy safeguards are greater than for any other platform.20,53
In my pilot work, I showed that OpenSAFELY can be used to evaluate the impact of the pandemic on long-
term conditions.1,2 I demonstrated that inflammatory arthritis diagnoses fell by 40% during the early pandemic,
while highlighting the remarkable adaptation of the NHS in the face of unprecedented pressures.1,2 These
analyses required no manual data collection; the number of captured diagnoses was 5-times greater than the
existing (manually-collected) National Audit programme;22 and these analyses can be updated regularly using
electronic dashboards linked to OpenSAFELY.
NHS England recently expanded OpenSAFELY’s use beyond COVID-19-related projects,23 making this
proposal timely and relevant. I will use this incredible resource to develop a data-driven approach to evaluating
care quality in near real-time. I will develop this methodology using the recently published NICE guideline for the
management of gout.11 By demonstrating this approach works for gout, the methodology will be applicable
to many other guidelines and conditions.
Gout is unique in that there are low-cost (£20 annually per patient), well-tolerated, urate-lowering medications
(ULT, e.g. allopurinol) that prevent symptoms, disability and complications.17,24 Guidelines recommend that ULT
should be offered to all people with gout,8,11 but care has remained woefully sub-standard. I demonstrated that
hospitalisations for gout have doubled in England since 2006, despite many of these admissions being
preventable with guideline-recommended treatments.14,25 In my Doctoral Fellowship, I showed that only 34% of
people with gout in the UK were prescribed ULT in 2022,2,10 while only 36% achieved the blood urate levels
necessary to prevent complications.10 NICE guidance for gout management was introduced in June 2022 with the
aim of improving care, but it remains unclear whether these recommendations are being followed. The
methodology I develop will enable us to evaluate this in near real-time, highlighting variation between regions and
patient groups, and quantifying the impact on health outcomes.
What is also clear from existing evidence is that guidelines need to be accompanied by effective
implementation strategies.10,26 I showed that guidelines from specialist societies (BSR, EULAR) had no
meaningful impact on care for people with gout in the UK.10 A recent systematic review highlighted that nurse-led
education approaches can improve gout care in randomised-controlled trial settings, assuming sufficient
available.17,21

challenge I encountered when trying to obtain NHS funding to sustain the nurse-led, hospital gout management
pathway I developed in my Doctoral Fellowship.
Some of the most effective strategies for implementing complex healthcare interventions are digital solutions that
streamline care and reduce unnecessary workload.27 Evidence from small studies outside the UK have shown
that electronic health record solutions (e.g. decision-support tools and electronic prompts) can improve outcomes
for people with gout.28-30 Despite their potential benefit and scalability, digital interventions to optimise gout
management have not been evaluated at scale in the NHS. I will address this knowledge gap using an
efficient, data-enabled trial design.
Traditional approaches to evaluating these interventions would be costly and resource-intensive. In 2021, NIHR
outlined the potential for routinely-collected data to increase the speed, efficiency and generalisability of trials.31 In
a recent proof-of-concept trial, a digital intervention designed to reduce the spread of COVID-19 was evaluated
using entirely routinely-collected data in OpenSAFELY.32 I will demonstrate how a similar, data-enabled study
design can evaluate quality improvement interventions in real-world care.
4. Aims and objectives
My hypothesis is that real-world data in the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment can be used to monitor
the implementation of NICE guidance, investigate drivers of healthcare inequality, and evaluate quality
improvement interventions efficiently.
I will complete three workstreams, using gout as an exemplar disease to address the following objectives:
1. Monitor the implementation of NICE guidance in near real-time, by mapping recommendations and
outcomes using health data for >99% of England’s population.
2. Develop a multi-faceted intervention to maximise the uptake of NICE guidance, incorporating digital
solutions and education.
3. Evaluate the implementation and effectiveness of this intervention using an efficient, data-enabled,
cluster-randomised trial in OpenSAFELY.
5. Project plan
Workstream 1 (months 0-12): Monitoring NICE guidance implementation
Aim
To develop a data-driven approach to monitoring real-world uptake of NICE guidance, highlighting variation in
care, and evaluating impact for patients.
I will develop this methodology using the NICE guideline for the management of gout. I am collaborating with
NICE to ensure the methodology is readily adaptable for other guidelines and diseases.
Methods
Data sources: I will use anonymised electronic health record data from primary and secondary care for >99% of
England’s population, linked securely via OpenSAFELY. I will publish my analysis code (Python/Stata/Markdown)
via GitHub to enable efficient re-use.
Study design: I will use an observational cohort design to evaluate the uptake and impact of NICE
recommendations for all people with recorded gout diagnoses in England.
To achieve this, I will:
1. Develop a variables library, by curating NHS data into simple, re-usable variables that make it easy to
identify someone with gout and associated treatments.
2. Systematically map recommendations from the NICE gout guideline,11 including:
i. Initiation of ULT;
ii. Serum urate monitoring;
iii. Attainment of urate targets.
3. Describe temporal trends in the proportion of patients who receive NICE-recommended gout care,
nationally and separated by:
i. Region;
ii. Age/sex;
iii. Ethnicity;
iv. Index of multiple deprivation quintile;
v. Comorbidity burden.
4. Use interrupted time-series and change-point detection methodology,33 to evaluate whether metrics
improved following the publication of NICE guidance.
5. Use regression-modelling approaches, including penalized regression (e.g. LASSO), to investigate
predictors of sub-optimal care, including characteristics associated with under-served groups:
i. Ethnic minority groups;
ii. Socioeconomically-disadvantaged groups;
iii. People with multiple long-term conditions.
6. Use multivariable Cox and Poisson regression to analyse whether guideline-recommended care
associates with improved outcomes, including:
i. Reductions in flares;
ii. Fewer consultations/hospitalisations;
iii. Reduced incidence/progression of gout-associated comorbidities, including kidney and
cardiovascular disease.
7. Generate publicly-available dashboards via OpenSAFELY Reports, to feedback performance against
NICE standards to clinicians, policy makers and patients.
8. Share this methodology with my collaborators at NICE, and adapt it for other guidelines.
9. Outline how a similar approach might work for other UK nations (e.g. via the SAIL Databank in Wales).
Sample size: I will include all people with incident or prevalent gout diagnoses. From my pilot analyses in
OpenSAFELY, the point prevalence and incidence of gout in 2023 were 3.2% and 1.44 per 1,000 adults,
respectively;2 equating to 1.45 million prevalent diagnoses and 65,120 annual incident diagnoses available for
analysis. This will provide 96% power to detect a 1% change in ULT initiation in the year pre- vs post-NICE
guideline implementation. Descriptive data will be reported with 95% confidence intervals for subgroups, including
by age, sex and ethnicity. For smaller sub-groups (e.g. women aged <60 years with gout), descriptive data will be
reported when the number of individuals is >400, which provides binomial exact confidence intervals with
maximum width +/- 5%.
Risk mitigation
Data access: I have already been granted approval by NHS England to analyse gout management using
OpenSAFELY. OpenSAFELY’s remit now covers non-COVID-19-related projects,23 and I have requested
approval to cover all analyses, with the support of my collaborators at OpenSAFELY.

Privacy: No individual patient data will be visible or transferred to Analysis code will be developed using
dummy data. Only summary-level outputs will be released following strict disclosivity checks. All platform activity
will be publicly-logged for transparency.
Code development: From my pilot work, I have developed analysis code/codelists for many of the variables I
require, for which there are low levels of missingness.
Personal development
To further my skills in computational analysis of complex health datasets, I will complete a part-time MSc in Health

Workstream 2 (months 12-24): Developing an intervention
Aim
To develop a multi-faceted intervention that provides NICE-recommended care for people with gout. I will use a
intervention,34,35
person-based approach to develop this following the principles outlined in the MRC/NIHR
complex intervention development framework.36 The intervention will be deliverable as part of routine care by
primary care clinicians, where the vast majority of gout is managed.
Methods
Stakeholders: I will establish a core stakeholder group, who I will consult throughout the stages listed below,
including people with gout, healthcare professionals, commissioners and third-sector representatives.
Focus groups: Three focus groups will be convened to encourage open discussion among those with shared
experiences and develop a shared understanding of the challenges of achieving optimal gout care:
 Focus group one will include people with gout, carers and relatives. I will use the links I have formed with
patient organisations (including the UK Gout Society) to purposively sample patients from diverse
backgrounds, including ethnic minority and under-served communities, older patients, and those with
multiple long-term conditions.
 Focus group two will include healthcare professionals from a broad range of localities and disciplines,
including GPs, rheumatologists, practice nurses, primary care-based pharmacists and physician
associates.
 Focus group three will include representatives from Integrated Care Systems, primary care software
providers and patient organisations, to explore how to embed interventions within existing NHS systems.
Focus groups will be held in-person, with 8-10 participants each, and facilitated by a trained patient and qualitative
methodologist. I will develop a topic guide with my patient advisory group, and use this to explore stakeholders’
experiences, including:
1. Barriers and facilitators of implementing treat-to-target ULT in primary care, particularly for under-served
groups;
2. Optimal interventions and implementation strategies to address identified barriers;
3. Whether these interventions are deliverable/scalable within existing NHS resources.
In-line with a person-based approach to intervention development, a set of guiding principles will be formulated
with stakeholders to summarise the ways in which the intervention can maximise engagement and support
context-specific behaviour change among healthcare professionals and patients.34,35 Logic models will be
developed, with underlying behaviours mapped to the COM-B framework;37,38 highlighting the inputs, activities and
mechanisms of change required to optimise outcomes (e.g. ULT initiation) and deliver impact for patients (e.g.
fewer gout flares).39
Intervention development: Through my collaborations with health psychologists and implementation science
experts (Centre for Implementation Science, KCL / Implementation Support Team, NICE), intervention
components with an existing evidence base will be adapted for use in the context of optimising gout
management.21 Contextual factors will be assessed using the Consolidated Framework for Implementation
Research (CFIR), with the CFIR-ERIC matching tool used to select appropriate implementation strategies.40
While the finalised intervention components will be selected during the development process, from my pilot work
with stakeholders I anticipate that the core components will include:
 Electronic health record solutions that make it easier for primary care clinicians to deliver NICE-
recommended gout care. This will utilise existing functionality within the software used in primary care in
England (SystmOne, EMIS, Ardens), and will not require new software development:
o NICE-aligned consultation templates and decision-support tools;
o Electronic prompts to management guidance and patient education material;
o Patient-recall reminders, to streamline the titration of ULT.
 Educational content for patients, empowering them to self-manage their gout:
o Videos on gout and its management, which I will develop with the UK Gout Society and British
Society for Rheumatology;
Signposting to patient information leaflets (Versus Arthritis) and e-Health diaries (e.g. Gout
o
Central).
 Electronic dashboards that provide feedback on performance against NICE-aligned quality metrics:
o National/regional benchmarking, using the OpenSAFELY dashboards from Workstream 1;
o Practice-level reports via SystmOne/EMIS/Ardens.
Care champions will be identified within practices to support intervention uptake among colleagues. Practices will
be encouraged to utilise allied health professionals (practice nurses, PCN pharmacists, physician associates),
where available, in the monitoring of urate levels and titration-to-target.17,21
Intervention piloting/refinement: Intervention components will be piloted with GPs, allied health professionals and
patients, using think-aloud interviews to generate a detailed understanding of user engagement.41 Potential
barriers to successful implementation will be identified, and intervention components modified to optimise their
utility and acceptability.
Risk mitigation
Development time: All digital intervention components will utilise functionality already available within primary care
software (SystmOne/EMIS/Ardens). This will greatly reduce development time and ensure familiarity amongst
clinicians. I have begun scoping work to develop these solutions (including NICE-aligned decision-support tools
with Ardens).
Stakeholder buy-in: I have built an extensive network of collaborators, including patients, primary/secondary care
clinicians, allied health professionals, and organisations including NICE, NHS England and Ardens, to maximise
input from a broad range of stakeholders.
Personal development
I will complete the Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions course at the National Centre for Research

Workstream 3 (months 24-60): Evaluating the intervention
Aim
To conduct an efficient, data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial in a primary care setting, to evaluate the
implementation and effectiveness of the intervention from Workstream 2. All study data will be automatically
captured in OpenSAFELY and analysed using the code I develop in Workstream 1. No manual data collection will
be required.
This methodology will be readily adaptable for other interventions and diseases, and will be shared openly
including with my collaborators at NICE.
Methods
Study design: Two-arm, parallel-group, cluster-randomised trial, with general practices as the unit of
randomisation. I have selected this study design as the intervention will be delivered at practice-level, reducing
treatment contamination between individuals.
Target population: People aged ≥18 years, registered with general practices in England, who consult a primary
care clinician for gout. Every patient consulting for gout at participating practices will be eligible; there will be no
exclusion criteria.
Intervention: Participating practices will be randomised 1:1 to receive:
The intervention package from Workstream 2; or

 Usual care.
The intervention will be delivered by primary care clinicians as part of routine care. It will serve as a guide to
delivering NICE-recommended gout care; however, all management decisions will be at the discretion of the
primary care team.
The intervention will be implemented remotely within participating practices: an initial training session will outline
how to use the intervention components to deliver NICE-recommended gout care; a follow-up session will be held
after two weeks, to consolidate training.
Randomisation/masking: Practices will be allocated by minimisation (conducted via the KCL Clinical Trials Unit),
stratified by region and practice list-size.42 Healthcare professionals will not be blinded to trial-arm allocation, nor
will study members who implement the intervention. After implementation, pseudonymised identifiers of
randomised practices will be linked securely to other data sources in OpenSAFELY. After linkage, the study team
will not be able to identify practices and will be blinded to outcomes.
management,11
Outcomes aligned to NICE recommendations for gout capturable using routinely-collected data
sources in OpenSAFELY, have been chosen:
Primary outcome:
 Proportion of people consulting for gout who newly initiate ULT (allopurinol, febuxostat, benzbromarone,
probenecid) within 6 months.
Secondary outcomes:
 Proportion of people who are adherent to ULT (proportion of days covered by ULT prescriptions ≥0.8)12;
 Proportion of people prescribed concomitant flare prophylaxis during ULT initiation/titration;11
 Proportion of people who attain serum urate levels ≤360 micromol/L within 6 months;11
 Rate of urate level monitoring;
 Rate of primary care consultations for gout flares;
 Rate of hospitalisations for gout flares;
 Proportion of people consulting for gout who develop incident myocardial infarction, stroke, CKD stages
3-5, or death due to these causes.
Safety outcomes:
 Proportion of people who develop incident Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis,
erythema multiforme or DRESS.8
Justification for primary outcome: ULT initiation is an important metric of whether NICE guidance has been
implemented, which is the primary objective of my intervention. It is reliably captured in primary care data, and
extensive evidence demonstrates that early ULT initiation improves patient outcomes.8,17,43-45 I considered other
primary outcomes with my collaborators. Clinical outcomes (e.g. gout flares) can take 1-2 years to improve after
ULT initiation, which would be less feasible as a primary outcome within the study timeframe.17,46 Urate target
attainment is also essential for optimal disease control;17 however, from my pilot work, 45% of patients did not
have urate levels performed within 6 months of ULT initiation,2 which could bias outcomes if this differed between
study arms. I have retained urate target attainment and flare rate as secondary outcomes, and I will explore how
to handle missing urate data using methods including multiple imputation.
Statistical analysis: Analyses will follow an intention-to-treat principle, and will include all individuals within
participating practices who consult for gout within 12 months of practice randomisation. Independence estimating
equations will be used to estimate treatment effects for primary and secondary outcomes, with weighting to
account for informative cluster size.47,48 Analyses will be adjusted for participant and cluster-level covariates,
including practice region and list-size. Sensitivity analyses will include estimations of cluster-average treatment
effects using unweighted cluster-level analysis, and mixed-effects models with general practice as a random
intercept.48
Sample size: From my pilot data in OpenSAFELY, there was a yearly average of 29 consultations for gout per
general practice in England between 2018-2024. The proportion of patients who newly-initiated ULT within 6
months was 40% (ICC 0.077). The recruitment of 38 practices (19 practices per arm) would provide 92% power
(alpha 0.05) to detect a 20-percentage-point increase in ULT initiation in the intervention group.
Feasibility statement: I sought extensive feedback from my collaborators and the NIHR RSS to inform my choice
of a data-enabled, cluster-randomised trial:
 Transferrable: This approach will serve as an efficient, adaptable method for evaluating other quality
improvement interventions across different diseases. It will provide real-world data on health outcomes
without any need for manual data collection.
 Precedent: Previous data-enabled, cluster-randomised trials in OpenSAFELY and CPRD have shown
approaches.32,49

throughout the UK.
 Pragmatic: My intervention will implement NICE-recommended care for a condition that is common but
poorly managed at baseline, using software (SystmOne, EMIS, Ardens) and treatments (e.g. allopurinol)
that primary care clinicians are familiar with.
Internal pilot: To mitigate against unexpectedly slow recruitment, I will conduct an internal pilot after recruiting 10
practices. A red/amber/green system will be used to determine whether to progress to the full study:
 Green: recruiting practices at 100% of target rate – continue recruitment;
 Amber: recruiting practices at 60-100% – explore mechanisms of increasing recruitment;
 Red: recruiting practices at <60% – stop recruitment, and proceed with pilot analyses.
If criteria for progression are not met, further recruitment will stop, and outcomes will be analysed for recruited
practices. These pilot data will inform future applications for funding, and enable me to modify my intervention and
assess methods of increasing practice engagement.
Nested qualitative implementation study: I have costed in the support of a post-doctoral research assistant with
qualitative expertise to conduct a nested qualitative study. This will provide more granular insight into
implementation outcomes, including users’ engagement with intervention components and modifications
necessary to support wider implementation. Patients and healthcare professionals will be purposively recruited
from participating practices with broad geographic and sociodemographic range, and invited to participate in semi-
guides,50
structured interviews. Normalisation Process Theory will inform interview topic written with my patient
advisory group. Approximately 10-15 patients and 10-15 healthcare professionals will be interviewed to allow data
saturation.51 Thematic analysis will be conducted on these data.52
Personal development
I will complete a training course on cluster-randomised trials (LSHTM), and undertake a research visit to the
University of Tsukuba, Japan, to learn from clinical epidemiologists and quality improvement experts in a different
healthcare system.
6. Dissemination, outputs and impact
Healthcare outputs: My analysis code and methodology will be shared openly, including with my collaborators at
NICE. They will serve as a transparent, adaptable framework through which:
1. Care quality can be monitored in near real-time across multiple disease areas;
2. Patient impact can be quantified - particularly for under-served groups, for whom data gaps exist
currently;
3. Strategies to deliver best practice care can be evaluated efficiently.
I will be working directly with NICE to focus implementation-support activities towards reducing healthcare
inequality.
Academic outputs: I will publish my findings from each workstream in high-impact, peer-reviewed journals with
wide audiences. I will present my findings at national and international conferences, including to primary care and
quality improvement audiences.
Patient outputs: By adopting a data-driven, implementation science approach, it will maximise the likelihood of my
outputs entering our health system and benefitting patients. I will use multiple approaches to inform study
participants, patients and the wider population, utilising collaborations with patient organisations (UK Gout
Society, Versus Arthritis), professional organisations (British Society for Rheumatology, Royal College of General
Practitioners) and public bodies (NICE, HQIP):
1. Plain language summaries to accompany all publications, co-written with my patient advisory group;
2. Social media summaries;
3. Public engagement events, via NIHR, RCGP and BSR networks;
4. Research open-days and science festivals;
5. Newsletters.
Impact statement: The goal of my Fellowship is to improve patient care by maximising the uptake of guideline-
recommended care:
 My research will directly benefit patients with gout, through reductions in pain, disability and
complications (e.g. erosive joint damage and kidney disease).
 For NHS clinicians, the intervention I develop will reduce unnecessary workload and serve as a
framework for delivering best practice care for other diseases.
 For commissioners, better disease control will reduce the number of avoidable consultations,
hospitalisations and complications.
 For quality assurers (e.g. CQC, HQIP), it will provide the information needed to highlight unwarranted
variation in care.
 For policy makers (e.g. NICE, NHS England), it will provide quantifiable evidence of patient impact, and
enable resources to be focused on areas where improvement is needed most.
 For researchers, my data-enabled trial design will serve as a pragmatic, efficient method for evaluating
interventions in real-world care.
7. Project Management
Project steering group: Annual meetings to provide strategic oversight and ensure successful delivery of my
project aims. Members will include my patient partners, mentors, Shaun Rowark (NICE representative), Professor
Abhishek (gout/trial expertise), Dr Chowla (GP), Dr Hudson (qualitative methodologist), Professor Norton
(statistician), and Dr Ellis (policy advisor to Versus Arthritis).
Patient advisory group: Meetings at the beginning of each workstream, then annually, to oversee PPIE strategy,
including co-developing educational materials, interpreting study results, and facilitating dissemination. Members
will include patients, lay members, patient organisation representatives, and a PPIE coordinator from KCL.
Trial steering committee: 6-monthly meetings during Workstream 3 to oversee trial conduct/progress. Members
will include an independent chair, statistician, GP, patient representative, and King’s CTU representative.
Data monitoring and ethics committee: To safeguard trial participants, including monitoring of safety outcomes.
Mentors: Bi-monthly meetings with Professors Galloway, Roddy and Coates to oversee my training and
development into an independent researcher.
Financial oversight with the KCL finance team to monitor my research budget.
8. Ethics
I have been granted approval by NHS England to conduct analyses of gout management using OpenSAFELY. I

I will submit ethics applications covering each workstream. I have experience of leading successful ethics

APPLICATION DETAILS.Patient & Public Involvement:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
To generate ideas for my Fellowship, I spoke with 10 people who had recently been treated for gout, using
semi-structured interviews to capture a diverse range of perspectives and priorities for improvement. The people I
spoke with highlighted several barriers to better gout care. This included a lack of information on the role of
preventative treatments; infrequent blood tests to monitor their urate levels; poor coordination of care; and a
stigma that gout is a self-inflicted disease due to bad diet. People from under-served groups, particularly those
with multiple long-term conditions, felt that their gout was viewed as unimportant, despite having a large impact on
their quality of life. These are issues that I have encountered repeatedly for people with gout during my clinical
career, despite numerous studies showing that the symptoms and complications of gout can be prevented
effectively with guideline-recommended treatments such as allopurinol.
To develop my proposal further, I convened a stakeholder meeting and a patient and public engagement
event, and collaborated with the leading patient organisation for people living with gout in the UK (the UK Gout
Society). The feedback received was incredibly informative: whereas my previous research had focused on
improving gout care in a hospital setting, it became clear that many of the difficulties patients continue to
encounter are in the community (where most gout care is provided). Patients reiterated that a single, focused
intervention would not address the many barriers to optimal gout care. Instead, a multi-faceted intervention,
incorporating both patient-centred education and clinician-facing solutions (e.g. digital solutions that streamline
care) would be essential. This has informed the focus and framework for my study plan.
Two public partners – Joel and Erman – who are experts by experience, have agreed to support me throughout
my Fellowship. They have extensive experience of the issues my research seeks to address, and their input has
been hugely beneficial in shaping this proposal, from study design to intervention ideas and dissemination plans.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
My public partners will be an integral part of my Fellowship. They will offer advice as members of my steering
group, and will be invited to join our research team. The Centre for Rheumatic Diseases has an established
process for arranging honorary research contracts and for supporting patient advisory groups. All patient
involvement will be supported by appropriate reimbursement, budgeted for in this proposal.
With the support of the UK Gout Society, I will invite additional public partners to form an advisory group, with
broad representation from under-served groups, lay members, patient organisations (including Dr Benjamin Ellis,
policy advisor to Versus Arthritis), and a patient and public involvement (PPI) coordinator from King’s. The group
will oversee PPI strategy, including ethics approval documentation, co-development of educational materials and
focus group topic guides, interpreting study results, dissemination planning, and a patient-led presentation at the
British Society for Rheumatology (BSR) conference.
Patients will be invited to facilitate focus groups to explore areas of good care and areas for improvement.
Training will be provided for patient facilitators, using the extensive PPI support available at King’s. I will invite
people with gout who participate in my study to a patient and public engagement event, to share our findings,
explore their perspectives, and identify areas for further improvement.
I am a trustee of the UK Gout Society, who will help me reach people with gout throughout the UK via their
membership, social media, and newsletters. In partnership with patients, the UK Gout Society and the BSR, I will
develop educational videos for people with gout, covering the condition, its treatment, and self-management.
These videos will form part of my study intervention, and will be publicised widely to ensure they benefit as many
people living with gout as possible.
To disseminate my findings to people with gout and the public, I will use several approaches:
 Plain language summaries accompanying all publications, co-written with my patient advisory group;
 Social media summaries of outputs, including via podcasts and X/Twitter;
 Public engagement events, utilising NIHR, RCGP and BSR networks;
 Research open-days and science festivals;
 Newsletters (e.g. via UK Gout Society).

APPLICATION DETAILS.Training & Development and Research Supp:
Proposed training and development programme
Training in skills necessary to support the proposed research
My NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and Clinical Lectureship have provided me with a strong foundation in big data
analysis, epidemiology and implementation science. An Advanced Fellowship would give me the opportunity to
develop new skills that will be incredibly helpful for this research proposal and in my future career as an
independent researcher in data-driven quality improvement:
 In Years 1 and 2, I will complete a part-time MSc in Health Data Science at the London School of
Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course aligns closely with the skills I will be using in Workstreams 1
and 3 of my proposal. It will be a fantastic opportunity to advance my skills as a health data scientist,
including modules dedicated to machine learning, predictive modelling and Bayesian analysis.
 In Year 2, I will complete the Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions course at the
National Centre for Research Methods, University of Southampton. This course is led by Dr Kathyrn
Skivington, lead author of the MRC/NIHR Complex Intervention Development Framework, which I will be
using to develop a complex intervention in Workstream 2 of my proposal. The skills I learn in this course
will complement the training I received in Health Behaviour Change (University of Oxford) and
Implementation Science (King’s College London) during my Doctoral Fellowship.
 In Year 3, I will complete the Design and Analysis of Cluster Randomised and Stepped Wedge Trials
course at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This course is led by Professor John
Bradley, an expert in cluster randomised trials. It covers many of the skills I will be utilising in Workstream
3 of my proposal, including cluster randomisation procedures, participant- and cluster-level analysis of
treatment effects, and ethical considerations in cluster randomised trials.
These courses will complement the training and expertise available to me at King’s College London, including
from experts in epidemiology (Professor James Galloway), data-enabled trial design (Professor Martin Gulliford),
experimental medicine (Professor Andrew Cope), statistics (Professor Sam Norton), implementation science
(Centre for Implementation Science), and PPIE (Dr Nicky Wilson).
Placements with other groups
During Year 1 of my Fellowship, I will complete a 3-month placement (1 day a week) with software developers
at the Bennett Institute for Applied Data Science, University of Oxford. This will give me the opportunity to
learn one-on-one from the team of professional coders, database engineers and data visualisation experts behind
the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Environment (led by Professor Ben Goldacre), which I will be utilising in
Workstreams 1 and 3.
Throughout my Fellowship, I will be working closely with the Data Analytics and Implementation Support teams
at NICE to develop the infrastructure needed to monitor the real-world uptake of clinical guidelines. This will be an
incredible learning and career development opportunity. It will enable me to influence the development and
implementation of national guidelines across numerous disease areas.
Training to support my development as a future health research leader
I will apply to the Emerging Research Leaders stream of NIHR’s Future-Focused Leadership programme,
which is designed specifically for post-doctoral researchers taking up their first significant leadership and
management roles. Through a mixture of residential workshops and training modules, I will be able to explore the
management and leadership skills that are essential for a successful career in health research, including the ability
to inspire other team members, build strong collaborations with external organisations, and the importance of
strategic awareness.
To further my skills in PPIE, I will complete a series of workshops at the National Coordinating Centre for Public
Engagement, including modules in community engagement and partnership working, in addition to the dedicated
PPIE training sessions at the NIHR BRC, University College London Hospitals.
I will take full advantage of the training opportunities available to health researchers at King’s College London,
including training programmes in Equality, Diversity and Inclusion, media engagement, and supervisory
skills.
Overseas research visit
In Year 4 of my Fellowship, I will complete a two-week placement at the University of Tsukuba, Japan, with
Professor Masao Iwagami and his team. This will give me the opportunity to learn about data-driven quality
improvement from clinical epidemiologists in an entirely different healthcare system, in a country that is renowned
for its approach to continuous quality improvement. I will apply this learning to my research proposal, and share it
with my collaborators at NICE, to build an understanding of how the UK can be an exemplar for data-driven quality
improvement.
Conferences
In each year of my Fellowship, I will attend at least one national conference to present my research findings and
learn from experts in the fields of rheumatology (British Society for Rheumatology Annual Conference), primary
care (Royal College of General Practitioners Annual Conference) and quality improvement (NHS Providers
Quality & Improvement Conference). I have also costed in attendance at two international conferences during my
Fellowship, to give me the opportunity to engage with leading health researchers from Europe and the United
States.
Planned clinical sessions
I am requesting 20% of my time to support two clinical sessions as a consultant rheumatologist. I will undertake a
weekly clinic in inflammatory arthritis at King’s College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, and establish a monthly,
multi-disciplinary, crystal arthritis clinic in collaboration with my colleagues at Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital
NHS Foundation Trust. This clinic will provide best practice care for people with gout and other crystal
arthropathies, which aligns closely with the aims of my Fellowship. There is a huge clinical need for this service,
with very few dedicated clinics in the UK for people with severe gout (e.g. those with end-stage renal disease,
severe tophaceous gout or recurrent hospitalisations). It will be fantastic opportunity to establish and lead such a
service.

Statement of support
We are delighted to support this exciting application, and to host Dr Mark Russell at the Centre for Rheumatic
Diseases at King’s College London.
Why I support Dr Russell
Mark is an excellent candidate for this NIHR Advanced Fellowship. Throughout his doctoral and post-doctoral
research, Mark has demonstrated incredible drive and motivation to learn new skills and collaborate. He is
conscientious, hard-working, and strives for the best, both in his academic work and for his patients. Mark’s

his 3-year NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, including first-author publications in The Lancet Rheumatology, The Lancet
Regional Health, and Annals of Rheumatic Diseases. He has been awarded national prizes, and was interviewed

early-career researcher. He has formed strong collaborations with organisations including NICE, the Healthcare

methodology, implementation science, and leadership and management. He has taken on teaching and
examination roles for our MBBS and Integrated Academic Training programmes, and he now plays a key role in
using large-language models to develop MBBS examination questions.
Mark’s recognition of the sub-optimal care offered to patients with gout has inspired him to use this condition to
develop the methodology in this Fellowship proposal. Gout is a disease that impacts enormously on patients, for
which there is a desperate need for better care, and for which Mark has a strong track-record in delivering
successful research. Mark was awarded the prestigious British Society for Rheumatology Young Investigator
Award for his demonstration of a large increase in hospitalisations for gout in England. He collaborated extensively

His research has directly benefitted patients, received national recognition, and has been adopted by other NHS
hospitals.
Mark’s Advanced Fellowship proposal has the potential to transform how we monitor the quality of care that is
delivered to patients. He will deliver infrastructure that does not exist currently: the ability to evaluate care quality in
near real-time. This is essential to improving patient outcomes and to organisations including NICE. Mark’s unique
skills in big data analysis and quality improvement methodology, combined with his clinical expertise, make him an
ideal person to lead this work.
Why King’s is the ideal host institution for Mark
The Centre for Rheumatic Diseases is a EULAR Centre of Research Excellence for rheumatology research. It has
been a launchpad for success for multiple clinical academics in the field of rheumatology and health services
research. Underlying the success of our department is cross-disciplinary leadership that bridges epidemiology,
clinical trials and qualitative methodology, integrated within NHS clinical practice. We have extremely supportive

prioritise training and career development over the quantity of research outputs, and we believe in the importance

Mark will benefit from our extensive experience of delivering high-quality health services research using routinely-
collected healthcare data. He will receive training and methodological support from experts in clinical epidemiology

Andrew Cope), health psychology (Dr Joanna Hudson), and patient and public involvement (Dr Nicky Wilson). He

Centre for Implementation Science at King’s, as well the support of a leading expert in data-enabled clinical trials

Mark will have access to a broad range of training in research methods, career development and mentorship
opportunities at King’s. Recognising that diversity of experience is essential at this stage of training, we actively

organisations including NHS England, the Healthcare Quality Improvement Partnership, and the British Society for

We have a strong track record in delivering bridging support to clinical researchers on completing fellowships. We
supported Mark in obtaining funding to bridge his transition from Doctoral Fellowship to Clinical Lecturer, and as a
co-applicant on NIHR HSDR and MRC programme grants. We are committed to supporting Mark during his
Advanced Fellowship, including through the provision of PhD students and research support. King’s also has a
Fellow-to-Faculty process which he would be eligible to pursue.
We are dedicated to ensuring equality of opportunity, recognising and celebrating our diversity and inclusion. We
have been a member of the Athena SWAN Gender Equality Charter since 2007, and King’s gained its most recent
Silver institutional award in 2021. We hold a Bronze Race Equality Charter Mark Award, and we are members of

(LGBTQIA+ equality). We are holders of the European Commission's HR Excellence in Research Award, in

I strongly believe that Mark is an excellent candidate for this NIHR Advanced Fellowship. It will provide him with
dedicated training and expertise in health data analysis and implementation science, and enable him to establish
